title: Read the room... and the person
description: Most of what people mean isn't in their words.
summaryPoints: Read body language and tone, not just the words — most of the message lives there
Trust clusters of cues, not one sign, and watch for changes from someone's normal
Listen to understand: don't draft your reply while they're still talking
Reflect back what you heard, then ask instead of assuming you know
When you're unsure what someone feels, the move is to check — not to mind-read

content.title: "I'm fine"
content.body:
Your friend says "I'm fine" — but their arms are crossed, their voice is flat, and they won't look at you. The words say one thing. Everything else says the opposite.

When they clash, the **body and tone** usually carry the truth. Most of what people actually mean never makes it into their words at all.
content.imageAlt: Two heads connected by a bridge, like seeing another person's side

content.title: The quiet channel
content.body:
Way more than words, people leak how they feel through:
- **tone of voice** — warm, sharp, flat, rushed
- **face** — a tight smile, a furrowed brow
- **posture** — leaning in, or turned away
- **eye contact** — steady, or dodging
- **pace** — clipped answers, long pauses

This is the channel most people forget to read. Tune into it and you'll catch what someone won't say out loud.

content.prompt: When someone's words and their tone clash, the words carry most of the emotional message.
content.explanation: It's the other way around — tone and body language carry most of the feeling. A flat "I'm fine" tells you more than the word "fine" does.

content.context: You ask a friend how their day went. They say "good," but it comes out flat, they're slumped, and they keep glancing at the door.
options: They're probably not actually good — the tone and posture say something's off
They're definitely angry at you specifically
They're bored by the question and nothing's wrong
content.explanation: The flat tone, slump, and glancing away all point the same way. You can't be sure what's wrong, but the cluster says don't take 'good' at face value.

content.title: Read clusters, not single cues
content.body:
One crossed arm? Maybe they're just cold. Don't build a whole story on a single sign.

Look for a **cluster** — several cues pointing the same way. Crossed arms **plus** a flat tone **plus** no eye contact **plus** short answers? Now you've got a real signal. One cue is noise. Three or four lining up is a message.
content.imageAlt: A body outline marked with emotion cues at the chest, jaw, and stomach

content.prompt: Which is the strongest evidence someone's upset?
options: Flat tone, no eye contact, one-word answers, and a tight jaw all at once
They check their phone for a second
content.explanation: Any single cue can mean nothing. Several cues stacking up in the same direction is what makes a read reliable.

content.instructions: One word makes this wrong. Tap it.
content.sentence: Trust a single cue and you will read people accurately.
content.explanation: One cue means little — read clusters of signals that point the same way, not a single gesture.

content.title: Watch for the change
content.body:
Everyone has a **baseline** — their normal. The real tell is when someone shifts away from it.

A friend who's usually loud in the group chat goes silent for a day. The classmate who always cracks jokes is suddenly short with everyone. The cue isn't the quiet or the snappiness itself — it's the **change** from how they normally are. That shift is your flag that something's up.
content.imageAlt: A lit match touching a fuse, like a noticeable change catching your attention

content.prompt: Match each cue to what it most likely signals.

pairs.left: Crossed arms and a turned-away body
A normally chatty friend goes quiet
Leaning in, nodding, steady eye contact

content.prompt: Open and engaged, or closed off?

content.title: The other half: actually listening
content.body:
Reading cues is half of it. The other half is **active listening**:
- **Give them your full attention** — phone down, eyes up
- **Don't draft your reply while they talk** — you'll miss everything
- **Reflect it back** — "sounds like you're slammed right now"
- **Ask, don't assume** — "what's going on?" beats guessing

Most people wait to talk instead of listening. Do the opposite.

content.prompt: Real listening, or just waiting to talk?

items.text: "So you felt left out — is that it?"
Planning your comeback while they speak
Putting your phone down to focus
Jumping in with your own bigger story

content.prompt: Reflecting back "sounds like you're ___" shows you actually heard them.
content.hint: Echo their feeling in your own words.
content.explanation: Reflecting back proves you were listening, not just waiting your turn — and lets them correct you if you read it wrong.

content.prompt: You think a friend seems off. What's the move?
options: Decide they're mad at you and start acting cold back
Say nothing and assume it'll pass
Check it: "you seem a bit off — everything okay?"
Announce that you know exactly what they're feeling
content.explanation: Reading people isn't mind-reading. A gentle check turns your guess into real information — and shows you noticed and care.

content.prompt: Put the steps for reading someone in order.
steps: Notice a cue — a flat tone or a turned-away body
Read the cluster — do several cues point the same way?
Check it by asking — "you seem off, everything okay?"
content.explanation: Notice, then confirm it's a real cluster, then ask. You never skip straight to assuming you know — you check.

content.prompt: Read what the OTHER person is probably feeling, and tap the right quadrant.

items.text: Your friend sprints over grinning — they just made the team
A classmate sits alone, quiet, staring out the window
Someone slams their locker and storms off down the hall
A friend is leaned back on the couch, relaxed and chatting easily
Your sibling is bouncing in their seat, talking fast about the concert tonight
A friend gives slow, flat one-word answers and won't meet your eyes

content.explanation: Same skill as reading yourself, aimed outward: energy (high or low) plus pleasantness (good or bad) places where someone's probably at — so you know how to meet them.
